reasoning: The Lean statement correctly encodes: P is monic cubic with integer coefficients a, b and constant -1; r is a real root; P is irreducible over ℚ; Q is monic cubic with integer coefficients c, d and constant +1; r+1 is a root of Q. The conclusion asserts existence of a root s of P equal to one of the two solution functions -1/(r+1) or -(r+1)/r — these are precisely the two "other roots" of P (they're legitimately the third root since the product of all roots is 1). The informal asks to "express another root as a function of r"; since there are two valid expressible roots, encoding the solution as a pair and asserting existence of a matching root is a natural and faithful encoding of this "exhibit a formula" style problem. The implicit constraint s ≠ r is automatically satisfied because neither f₁(r)=r nor f₂(r)=r admits a real solution (both reduce to r²+r+1=0).
